request.question: Among film directors, who was more well-known for horror films, Eric Red or Howard Hughes?

title: Summa Corporation
Bangladesh Film Directors Association
Altic/Howard Hughes (METRORail station)
Eric Red
The Tights
The Amazing Howard Hughes
List of Cambodian films of 1966
Howard Hughes Corporation
Hughes XF-11
Howard Hughes
sentences: Summa Corporation was the name adopted for the business interests of Howard Hughes after he sold the tool division of Hughes Tool Company in 1972.
The tool division would merge with Baker International in 1987 to form Baker Hughes, the world's third-largest oil-services company.
The name "Summa", Latin for "highest", was allegedly chosen by several of Hughes' employees without consulting him first.
Hughes was allegedly dissatisfied, and preferred the name "HRH Properties" - with the initials standing for both "Howard Robard Hughes" and "Hughes Resort Hotels".
His suggestions were never implemented, and the company remained Summa for 18 years following Hughes' death.
Bangladesh Film Directors Association is the pan-national trade body of film directors in Bangladesh.
The Bangladesh Film Directors Association’s General Secretary is Badiul Alam Khokon.
Mushfiqur Rahman Gulzar is the president of Bangladesh Film Directors Association.
Altic/Howard Hughes is a light rail station in Houston, Texas on the METRORail system.
It is served by the Green Line and is located on Harrisburg Boulevard at Altic Street in the East End.
The station is named for Altic Street as well as Howard Hughes, who built a Hughes Tool Company factory in the area.

Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".
The Tights are an English punk rock band from Worcester.
The band's only two singles were released by the Cherry Red Records label: "Bad Hearts" / "It" / "Cracked" (1978) and "Howard Hughes" / "China's Eternal" (1978).
After John Peel played "Bad Hearts" on BBC Radio 1, this single made it to number six in the UK punk chart.
Follow-up "Howard Hughes" reached number four in the same chart.
After playing their farewell gig at the Marquee Club, London, The Tights split in 1979.

The Amazing Howard Hughes is a 1977 American made-for-television biographical film about American aviation pioneer and filmmaker Howard Hughes, based on the book "Howard: The Amazing Mr. Hughes" by Hughes' business partner Noah Dietrich.
The film starred Tommy Lee Jones, Ed Flanders, and Tovah Feldshuh and was made a year after Hughes's death, in 1976, while aboard a private flight to Mexico.
It was originally broadcast in two parts on CBS on April 13 and 14, 1977.

Ros Serey Sothear becomes a popular artist for movie soundtracks among film directors, taking Huoy Mea's old position Of the 18 films listed, 4 films are in existence, 6 have been remade, and 8 have not yet been remade.
The Howard Hughes Corporation () is a major real estate development and management company based in Dallas, Texas.
It began as part of the oil drilling tool business founded by Howard R. Hughes, Sr., in 1913, which under his son Howard Hughes, Jr., diversified into real estate development.
The real estate operations became a separate company, which was acquired by The Rouse Company, which in turn was acquired by General Growth Properties (GGP).
In 2010, GGP formed The Howard Hughes Corporation as a separate company.
The Hughes XF-11 was a prototype military reconnaissance aircraft, designed and flown by Howard Hughes and built by Hughes Aircraft for the United States Army Air Forces.
Although 100 F-11s were ordered in 1943, only two prototypes and a mockup were completed.
During the first XF-11 flight in 1946, Howard Hughes crashed the aircraft in Beverly Hills, California.
The production aircraft had been cancelled in May 1945, but the second prototype was completed and successfully flown in 1947.
The program was extremely controversial from the beginning, leading the U.S. Senate to investigate the F-11 and the Hughes H-4 Hercules flying boat in 1947–1948.

Howard Robard Hughes Jr. (December 24, 1905 – April 5, 1976) was an American businessman, investor, pilot, film director, and philanthropist, known during his lifetime as one of the most financially successful individuals in the world.
He first made a name for himself as a film producer, and then became an influential figure in the aviation industry.

Later in life, he became known for his eccentric behavior and reclusive lifestyle—oddities that were caused in part by a worsening obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) and chronic pain from a plane crash.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Among film directors, who was more well-known for horror films, Eric Red or Howard Hughes?
hop1_docs: Eric Red | Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".

Eric Brian Hughes | Eric Brian Hughes also known as E.B. Hughes, is an American writer, director and producer.  He has written, produced and directed two feature films, "Pacing the Cage" (2014) and "Turnabout" (2016).  His screenplay "The Fallen Faithful" won Best Screenplay at the 2011 Beverly Hills Film Festival.  Hughes is a member of the Producers Guild of America.

Howard Deutch | Howard Deutch (born September 14, 1950) is an American film and television director.  He is perhaps best known for his collaboration with filmmaker John Hughes, having directed two of Hughes' well-received screenplays, "Pretty in Pink" and "Some Kind of Wonderful".  Since 2010, he has primarily directed television productions, including multiple episodes of "Getting On" and "True Blood".

The Creature Walks Among Us | The Creature Walks Among Us, released in 1956, is the third and final installment of the "Creature from the Black Lagoon" horror film series from Universal Pictures, following 1955's "Revenge of the Creature".  The film was directed by John Sherwood, the long-time Universal-International assistant director, in his directorial debut.  Jack Arnold, who had directed the first two films in the series, had moved on to "A-list" films, and felt he had no more to contribute to the horror genre.  He suggested that his assistant director, Sherwood, could move up to full director, which partly affected Universal's decision to allow him to direct the film.  "The Creature Walks Among Us" starred Jeff Morrow, Rex Reason, and Leigh Snowden, and, like the original "Creature from the Black Lagoon", had music composed by Henry Mancini, who at the time was under contract with Universal.

Vernon Zimmerman | Vernon Zimmerman is an American writer and director who made his feature debut as director with the 1962 short "Lemon Hearts" starring Andy Warhol associate Taylor Mead.  Zimmerman wrote and directed the Claudia Jennings roller derby drive-in film "Unholy Rollers" and collaborated with Terrence Malick on the script to the road movie "Deadhead Miles".  He is most well known for his horror slasher film "Fade to Black", a dark and despairing psychological study of an awkward and alienated hardcore film buff who exacts a harsh revenge on his cruel tormentors.  Zimmerman received a Saturn Award nomination as Best Director for the film, a predecessor to more well-known modern parodies of the horror genre.  Vernon also wrote the scripts for the horror-Western "Hex" (a.k.a. "Charmed"), the redneck crime exploitation film "Bobbie Jo and the Outlaw", the made-for-TV wrestling comedy/drama "Mad Bull", the failed TV pilot film "Shooting Stars", and the teen fantasy comedy "Teen Witch".  Zimmerman's latest film is the six-minute comic short "Chuck and Wally on the Road".  More recently Vernon has been working as a script analyst.  He also teaches screen-writing courses at UCLA's Extension and Certificate Program.  Zimmerman also taught classes on both writing feature scripts and directing actors for film and television at the USC School of Cinema and Television.  Vernon Zimmerman lives in Los Angeles and is a member of both the Writers Guild of America and the Directors Guild of America.

Inside (2007 film) | Inside (French: "À l'intérieur" ) is a 2007 French home invasion horror film directed by Julien Maury and Alexandre Bustillo, starring Alysson Paradis and Béatrice Dalle.  It was written by co-director Bustillo, and is the first feature film from either director.  It concerns the attack and home invasion of a young pregnant woman by a mysterious stranger who seeks to take her unborn baby.  The film received generally positive reviews from mainstream critics upon its release and was particularly well received among horror film critics, noting it for being a genuinely scary and brutally violent example of the new wave of French horror.

Voice (film) | Voice ( also known as Whispering Corridors 4: Voice and Voice Letter) is a 2005 South Korean horror film, and the fourth installment of the "Whispering Corridors" film series.  This film was the debut film for its three young actresses, as well as director Choi Ik-Hwan, who had served as an assistant director on the first film of the series.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Eric Red — [1] "best known for writing the horror films \"The Hitcher\" and \"Near Dark\""  
- Eric Brian Hughes — [2] "also known as E.B. Hughes"  
- Howard Deutch — [3] "is an American film and television director."

Ambiguities:
- "Howard Hughes" — NOT present in passages; could be confused with "Howard Deutch" ([3] "is an American film and television director.") or with other "Hughes" entries such as "Eric Brian Hughes" ([2] "also known as E.B. Hughes").
- "Eric" name variants — two different entries: "Eric Red" ([1]) vs "Eric Brian Hughes" ([2]).

Bridge / missing fact:
- Which specific "Howard Hughes" is meant (page/entity) and whether that Howard Hughes is a film director.
- Whether that Howard Hughes is known for horror films (genre association / "best known for" attribution).
- Comparative statement: explicit evidence that Howard Hughes is (or is not) known for horror to directly compare with Eric Red's documented horror reputation.

Retrieval direction:
1) Open "Howard Hughes (disambiguation)" or run query: "Howard Hughes (disambiguation) site:wikipedia.org" to locate any film-director entry.  
2) Open the specific page for a candidate director: "Howard Hughes (film director)" or search: "Howard Hughes film director Wikipedia".  
3) If the intended person is the producer/industrialist, fetch "Howard Hughes (businessman) filmography" or "Howard Hughes film producer Wikipedia" to check genre associations.  
4) Confirm Eric Red page if needed: "Eric Red - Wikipedia" (already summarized in [1]).  
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Eric Red | Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".

Some Kind of Wonderful (film) | Some Kind of Wonderful is a 1987 American romance film starring Eric Stoltz, Mary Stuart Masterson, and Lea Thompson.  It is one of several successful teen dramas written by John Hughes in the 1980s, although it was directed by Howard Deutch rather than Hughes.
Night of the Wild | Night of the Wild is a 2015 American TV horror movie directed by Eric Red.

Howard Hughes Corporation | The Howard Hughes Corporation () is a major real estate development and management company based in Dallas, Texas.  It began as part of the oil drilling tool business founded by Howard R. Hughes, Sr., in 1913, which under his son Howard Hughes, Jr., diversified into real estate development.  The real estate operations became a separate company, which was acquired by The Rouse Company, which in turn was acquired by General Growth Properties (GGP).  In 2010, GGP formed The Howard Hughes Corporation as a separate company.

The Amazing Howard Hughes | The Amazing Howard Hughes is a 1977 American made-for-television biographical film about American aviation pioneer and filmmaker Howard Hughes, based on the book "Howard: The Amazing Mr. Hughes" by Hughes' business partner Noah Dietrich.  The film starred Tommy Lee Jones, Ed Flanders, and Tovah Feldshuh and was made a year after Hughes's death, in 1976, while aboard a private flight to Mexico.  It was originally broadcast in two parts on CBS on April 13 and 14, 1977.

The Tights | The Tights are an English punk rock band from Worcester.  The band's only two singles were released by the Cherry Red Records label: "Bad Hearts" / "It" / "Cracked" (1978) and "Howard Hughes" / "China's Eternal" (1978).  After John Peel played "Bad Hearts" on BBC Radio 1, this single made it to number six in the UK punk chart.  Follow-up "Howard Hughes" reached number four in the same chart.  After playing their farewell gig at the Marquee Club, London, The Tights split in 1979.

Howard R. Hughes Sr. | Howard Robard Hughes Sr. (September 9, 1869 – January 14, 1924) was an American businessman and inventor.  He was the founder of Hughes Tool Company.  He invented the "Sharp–Hughes" rotary tri-cone rock drill bit during the Texas Oil Boom.  He is best known as the father of Howard Hughes, the famous American business tycoon.
response.summary_2: Eric Red is best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", and there is no supporting evidence in the provided passages that Howard Hughes is known for horror films.
